data.name: Alisson
data.description: Goalkeeping is in the family’s DNA. Alisson’s great grandfather played as a keeper in his hometown club and his father wore the gloves in every match with his friends. His mother was also a goalkeeper, but in handball. And his older brother, Muriel, showed Alisson the way by turning pro and being a role model for him at Internacional. Alisson joined Roma in 2016 and has been at Liverpool since 2018, winning every possible trophy, including the Champions League. Religion plays a big part in his life, and there are videos of him baptising Roberto Firmino and Fred's wife. "There is a small distinction: I’m not religious, I’m a follower of Christ," he told Folha de São Paulo in 2020. "When we talk about religion, it’s very much tied to traditions and factors that somewhat tarnish the rich history of Christianity. Jesus is much more than religion.”

data.name: Gabriel Magalhães
data.description: Now one of the world’s best centre-backs, Gabriel had a stuttering start to life in football. As a young teenager he left his São Paulo home to join Avaí’s academy, but returned after a week with homesickness. After speaking to his father, Gabriel called Avaí's coach, Diogo Fernandes, who agreed to have him back. “We knew this could happen as the first problem a footballer faces is being far away from their family,” Diogo said in 2020. “We also knew that Avaí would be one of the few clubs that would accept Gabriel. He was skinny and very tall, a left-footer, but he was a bit soft in challenges and couldn’t head the ball too well. We were right in the end, though: he was the biggest transfer in Avaí history [when sold to Lille in 2017] and a superb player … He wasn’t built by talent, but by his will to improve his shortcomings and learn that he could surpass any difficulties life throws at him. We knew he could be a top player from the start.” After three years in France, he joined Arsenal for £27m. Known at the Emirates as Big Gabi, to avoid confusion with namesake Martinelli.

data.name: Marquinhos
data.description: Described as “a leader on and off the field” by his club manager, Luis Enrique, Marquinhos has been one of the best in his position for more than a decade, shining since he joined PSG from Roma in 2013. As a boy he enjoyed playing in goal; marshalling the defence has always been in his nature. Now, as Ancelotti’s international captain, he will be in charge of lifting the trophy if Brazil win it this summer. He had won 10 Ligue 1 titles by the time he finally laid hands on the Champions League trophy – for the first time – last year, and praised Luis Enrique for making it happen. "He is certainly one of the best coaches in the world, and he shows that to us every day. He is a very demanding coach, very clear with the players, and he always brings something extra.”

data.name: Casemiro
data.description: One of Real Madrid’s all-time greats. When he moved to Manchester United in 2022 he was asked by reporters about one of the many new things he would have to get used to, namely no Champions League football. Was it something that bothered him? “Well, I have five already,” he smiled. Since then, his time at United has been mixed: a sharp decline, but, this season – his last at United – a major recovery, finding form just at the right time for this summer. Ancelotti told reporters last year: “There are no players in Brazil with the same characteristics as Casemiro.” He is known too for quiet leadership and told the Guardian in 2020: “It’s just my personality. I am very, very, very calm, even if my playing style is aggressive and everyone can see that. It’s my mum’s values: be polite, treat everyone the same. You can’t lose that, ever. You don’t need to shout to lead.” His correct name is Casimiro; he became, and stuck with, "Casemiro" instead when São Paulo spelt his name wrong on his shirt. “I played very well that day and I’m a very superstitious person, so I told the club: ‘There’s no need to change it.’ It was a one-match mistake; now I’ll carry it with me for ever."

data.name: Alex Sandro
data.description: No matter where he goes, Alex Sandro collects trophies. After Athletico Paranaense (2008-10), Santos (2010-11), Porto (2011-15) and Juventus (2015-24), he came to Flamengo in 2024 and since then he has won every trophy there is to win in South America. He owes much of his speed, precision and technical focus to having spent plenty of time as a youngster doing capoeira, the traditional Brazilian non-contact martial art. “Even in Italy some coaches told me that some of the movements resemble capoeira,” he said. “My mother did it, my brother too; we have a history within it.” He always had a solid work ethic too: as a boy he sold CDs and DVDs on the streets. His brother Flavio told Globo in 2022: “It was his way to get money to buy candy or whatever else he wanted.”

data.name: Vinícius Júnior
data.description: Almost everyone in Brazil knew what was coming from Viní Júnior, right from the start. The hype began when he was a pre-teen at Flamengo, and he delivered. In 2017, at 17, he agreed a £39m move to Real Madrid, where he grew into the symbol of the team’s success post-Cristiano Ronaldo. Form hasn’t always been consistent, including with the Seleção, but if there is one man who knows how to get the best out of him it is Carlo Ancelotti. In 2024, Vinícius said of his then club manager: “He's done everything for me. He's always given me confidence, told me off when I needed it. He cares about me as much as I care about him. He's changed me as a player, not on the pitch, but in the way I think.” He has repeatedly been the victim of racist abuse in La Liga, and has repeatedly spoken out against it. He said in 2024: “I just want to play football but it’s hard to move forward … I feel less and less like playing. But it never crossed my mind [to leave Spain] because if I leave Spain I give the racists exactly what they want.”

data.name: Bruno Guimarães
data.description: At the age of three Guimarães was in hospital with pneumonia, and again two years later. Doctors encouraged his parents to get him active to help his recovery and strengthen his lungs. First they tried swimming, but he didn’t like it. Then came football, and he didn’t look back. It was a tough road to the top of the game, though. At 16 he joined Audax in São Paulo, where, he told UOL in 2022, “I shared a room with 22 other boys, some rats, and a few leaks”. He severely doubted himself at Audax, crippled by nerves, and said he owed everything to his coach there, Fernando Diniz. “I used to be terrified of playing soccer, feeling sick and even vomiting before games, skinny as a rail, with no strength whatsoever, listening to Diniz tell me: 'You're going to be a spectacular player.' And I just thought: 'This guy is crazy.' He told me I would be good at any profession I chose because I was dedicated and determined … He's a unique guy, a fundamental person. I root for him and we're still in touch.” Guimarães’s club future is unclear amid links to Manchester United and Chelsea.

data.name: Matheus Cunha
data.description: Cunha was used regularly by Tite during the 2022 World Cup cycle, but, left on the fringes of the Atlético Madrid first team, missed the cut for Qatar. “I felt bad, really bad,” he told the Guardian last year. “I suffered a lot. I felt bad not to have played the World Cup and I felt even worse because I didn’t go to the World Cup because I wasn’t playing for my club.” Moving to Wolves turned things round for him. “Sometimes all we want in life is affection. People think we have everything, but we’re human beings, too. We need a bit of understanding – we have our difficulties. Wolves gave me that joy back.” He played his way back into international contention, earning last year’s £62.5m move to Manchester United. Carlo Ancelotti is a big fan of his style and versatility, so he could become Brazil’s fifth different No 9 in the last five World Cups – a position without a regular owner since Ronaldo stopped playing.

data.name: Neymar
data.description: Seventy-nine goals in 128 international appearances: a formidable record for a striker at his fourth World Cup – but it’s obvious that the 34-year-old is a shadow of the player he once was. And that’s before you consider the calf injury he picked up just before the tournament, which will leave him, at best, lacking full match fitness in the early stages. He hasn’t played for the national team since 2023 due to injuries and patchy form since his return to Santos, but was still given the nod by Carlo Ancelotti ahead of rivals such as João Pedro and Richarlison, seemingly because of his aura. "I know full well that Neymar is much loved, not only by the public but also by the players," Ancelotti said. "This is ​a factor, because we have to consider the atmosphere that will surround Neymar's call-up.” He remains defiant in the face of criticism, and continues to embrace his off-pitch celebrity status. In 2020 he showed off his acting abilities when he made a cameo as a monk named João in episodes six and eight of his favourite Netflix series, La Casa de Papel, in 2020.

data.name: Raphinha
data.description: As a teenager, Raphinha almost gave up on the sport. He started out playing in a social project club in Porto Alegre and was frequently dismissed for being too skinny, failing a series of club trials, shaking his confidence. But eventually he was scouted by the Avaí academy and moved from there to Portugal’s Vitória Guimarães, where he made his professional breakthrough. Since then, via spells in France, England and now at Barcelona, he has steadily developed into one of the fastest, most threatening wide attackers in the game, a nightmare for opponents. He finished 2024-25 with 34 goals scored and 22 assists in 57 games, but only fifth in the Ballon d'Or rankings, which didn’t go down well. “I think I deserved to be first for what I delivered during the season, for the titles I won, for the numbers I achieved, and for everything I contributed on the pitch. I think I deserved to win."

data.name: Weverton
data.description: One of Carlo Ancelotti's many surprise picks in this final squad, Weverton turns 39 in December. Why did he get the nod over younger rivals? Ancelotti said simply: “In some positions, like goalkeeper, we prioritise experience.” Choosing a seasoned pro as backup may have been prompted by concerns over Alisson's fitness and Ederson’s patchy form going into the tournament, and he will be an able backup option if he’s called upon in what will be his second World Cup. He was a key part of Palmeiras’s success from 2018 to earlier this year when he moved to Grêmio on a three-year deal, though age has started to show – and he has never been an expert penalty saver. His reaction to being called up went viral online: a video showing him appearing to faint when hearing his name. “My heart was racing,” he explained later. “Jokes aside, my fall was from relief, emotion, and everything that goes through your mind during that moment.”

data.name: Danilo
data.description: One of the few players to have won both the Champions League and the Copa Libertadores titles. Last year his header secured Flamengo their fourth Libertadores in the final against Palmeiras. A right-back as a youngster, he adapted to the centre during his years at Juventus, and is now a rock in Flamengo’s backline, and their captain. Being a leader is something he has done since childhood when he took care of his three younger brothers while his father, a truck driver, and mother, a housekeeper, were away. "I prepared a feeding bottle for each one, because after the bottle they would fall asleep. I babysat all of them a lot," he told Globo TV. One of the brothers, Dener, said: “Danilo has always been that super-protective guy. Giving advice, scolding … He has also always been very studious, always top of his class.” The multilingual Danilo still prefers reading books to playing video games. “I read a little bit of everything, but what I like most are books that talk about human interactions, relationships, and stories of overcoming challenges and resilience.”

data.name: Bremer
data.description: Gleison Bremer’s dad liked to name his children after important people. One of Bremer’s brothers is called Kennedy after John F, while Bremer was named after Germany’s 1990 World Cup final goalscorer Andreas Brehme. “There are three brothers in the family and we were all named after someone important, revered or admired,” Bremer told the Telegraph in 2023. “Andreas Brehme was a player my father liked very much.” When Brehme died in 2024, Bremer posted online: “You were my dad's idol. For me, you will always remain an example of sportsmanship.” Bremer joined Juventus from Torino in 2022, making his Brazil debut the same year. Dominant, speedy and fierce, he says he owes a lot of qualities to playing street football as a kid. “I learned how not to lose. I learned how to be aggressive in the streets. I also learned how to be a little bit sneaky.” When he wasn’t playing football or studying as a boy, he was helping his family on the farm or selling ice cream. “My mother used to make the ice creams, and I would help her put them in the freezer so I could sell them the next day, which was market day. She would give me half of what I made so I could go play soccer in the neighbouring town, or play video games," he told ESPN.

data.name: Léo Pereira
data.description: The last five years at Flamengo have been a rollercoaster for Léo Pereira. Stuck on the bench, he considered leaving the club, but, following Dorival Jr’s arrival for a third spell as coach, he grew into one of the pillars of a hugely successful team. The former Flamengo manager Filipe Luís said last year: "To me, he is the best centre-back playing in Brazil." Pareira earned his first cap in March this year, starting against France, and while his performance wasn't great, with the first goal originating from him losing a battle with Kylian Mbappé, his ability to start attacking moves from the back, his precise passing and good positioning convinced Carlo Ancelotti he deserved a place in this summer’s squad. “My time at Flamengo has been marked by my resilience and by the fact that I, my family, and those closest to me never stopped believing,” he said after his first call-up. “I had a bad start at Flamengo but, from 2022 onward, I managed to find very good consistency and win several titles with my teammates.”

data.name: Douglas Santos
data.description: The 32-year-old left-back has had an on-off relationship with the Brazil national setup. He earned his first call-up back in 2013 but struggled to build on that, and, in March last year, having played for Zenit St Petersburg since 2019 and being given Russian citizenship in 2024, was on the verge of switching his international allegiance to Russia – until he was put on Brazil’s provisional squad list for the World Cup qualifiers. “I’ve always had a lot of respect for Russia, as it’s the country where I have built so much of my career,” he told Fifa.com in May. “But wearing the Brazilian shirt has always been the dream, so that took priority.” His inclusion in this squad is the realisation of a lifelong dream. “My first clear memory is of the World Cup in 2002, watching the matches with my family, as well as the anticipation before each game. That really marked my childhood.”

data.name: Fabinho
data.description: Since joining the Portuguese club Rio Ave at 18, Fabinho has lived outside Brazil, and has been in the Saudi Pro League captaining Al-Ittihad since 2023. The most successful years of his career were at Liverpool, winning the Champions League in his first season and the Premier League title in his second. His decision to leave three years ago, after 219 appearances in five years, surprised many. “Jürgen Klopp called me and he asked me what I wanted,” Fabinho says. “I said that I’d heard the offer and liked it but that I wanted to hear from him what he thought. He said that if there are any players who want to leave, he won’t hold them back. It was a speech that kind of opened the door for me to leave. I left in peace and happy with my decision.” He brings a wealth of experience and leadership to the squad this summer. When he got the call, he wrote online: “Another opportunity to live one of the greatest honours of my life: to play a World Cup for my country. Today and always grateful to God! Let's go!"

data.name: Danilo Santos
data.description: Back in 2020, the Palmeiras Under-20s were training against the first team’s reserves, watched by the first-team head coach, Vanderlei Luxemburgo. A skinny 18-year old quickly caught his eye. "He was everywhere,” said the former Brazil manager Luxemburgo. “He didn't pass backwards or sideways, just to the front. I thought: ‘Holy shit, what a player.’ I wanted him.” Danilo made his first-team debut shortly after he was signed by Nottingham Forest in 2022 for a reported £16m. But on the opening day he broke his ankle, meaning he featured just 13 times in that first season. Botafogo brought him back to Brazil for £21m in 2025, and, in March this year, he won his first cap in the Carlo Ancelotti era. In May he talked about why he always seems so composed in the middle. “When I’m on the pitch, I don’t think about anything else,” he told Fifa.com. “I feel completely free. It doesn’t matter what’s happening in the match, who we’re playing against, whether we’re playing well or not. I always feel at ease – like I’m having a kickabout back in Fazenda Coutos, just enjoying a game, playing with my dog, Pit. Everything feels very natural out there.”

data.name: Endrick
data.description: It’s been a rollercoaster few years for Endrick. At 16 the Palmeiras prodigy signed a pre-contract deal with Real Madrid in a €70m move, dubbed the greatest Brazilian prospect since Neymar. In his first season there, when he turned 18, he had ups and downs, showing flashes of brilliance. But then it turned sour: Carlo Ancelotti left, Endrick was injured, and when he returned to fitness, the new coach, Xabi Alonso, barely used him at all. It was a reassuring talk with Ancelotti, by then Brazil’s coach, which lifted his spirits; when he joined Lyon on loan last year Endrick said Ancelotti had “told me to go out, play and develop your skills where you can play and be happy". In France, he has rebuilt his confidence and showed off those qualities, forcing his way back into the Brazil setup. In 2023 he talked about handling criticism. “My mother and my sister say that I’m cold. People say I have a heart of ice and I’m very cold with the decisions I make and what I say … I used to be angry and try to counter the criticism. But then I understood that I didn’t need to prove it, I didn’t need to show the opposite. I’m Endrick. If they want to insult me, I won’t see it.”

data.name: Lucas Paquetá
data.description: “I don’t think Flamengo needed me, but I needed Flamengo.” Paquetá, having been cleared last year of spot-fixing after a two-year investigation, was determined to leave West Ham and go home. The stress he was under was evident in November when he was shown two yellow cards, both for dissent, in a defeat by Liverpool. He later responded online to a clip of the Sky pundit Rob Green accusing him of “ridiculous behaviour” by writing: “It’s ridiculous to have your life and career affected for two years without any psychological support from the federation. Perhaps this ridiculous behaviour is just a reflection of everything I’ve had to endure and, it seems, have to continue enduring! I’m sorry if I’m not perfect.” When he arrived back in Brazil, with his £35.5m move from West Ham to Flamengo the most expensive incoming transfer in the country’s history, he said: “I've dreamt so much about this moment. All I want is to enjoy this happiness of finally being home. I want to be happy, bring joy to the nation, and give my best.” His ability to dictate play as a box-to-box midfielder makes him a one-of-a-kind player in Ancelotti’s squad, and sure of a starting spot if fit.

data.name: Luiz Henrique
data.description: A potent gamechanging option from the bench for Carlo Ancelotti, who said before the tournament: “Luiz Henrique has extraordinary talent. He's very strong physically, fantastic in one-on-ones.” The speedy winger left Botafogo for Zenit St Petersburg in January last year. He’s known for his Black Panther mask goal celebration, which dates back to his time at Betis when one of the coaches said he looked like the Marvel superhero. “This idea appealed to my friend, who composed a song for me and suggested that I wear a mask and display the Black Panther gesture during celebrations,” he told the Russian paper Izvestia last year. “He emphasised that it would catch on, kids and fans would love it.” But it’s not his favourite superhero. “I really like the Flash, because he's so fast. I'm also often called fast. So out of all the superpowers, I would choose the speed that Flash has.” He also loves Lionel Messi, and named his dog after him. “Yes, it's true. I named him Messi because he is also light and, in a good way, he reminds me of him.”

data.name: Gabriel Martinelli
data.description: Martinelli has already helped Brazil Under-23s win gold at the Olympics. Can he now help end Brazil’s spell of no World Cup titles in 24 years? This is his second chance, having also featured in Qatar. He arrives in the US on the back of a title-winning club season, and remains as cool and composed as ever, brushing off pressure. In 2022 he reflected on his childhood, saying how he loved football, but rarely dreamed of making it as a professional – until, that is, the World Cup came round. “When the flag is painted on the front of your house,” he wrote in the Players' Tribune, “when you have 15 family members in your backyard, the TV is rolled out, there are balloons, fireworks, honking horns, your cousins are running up the stairs and your uncle is at the barbecue … how can you not? You are watching the Seleção and you’re thinking: ‘Imagine wearing that shirt at a World Cup.'”

data.name: Ederson
data.description: Playing with Ederson is like having an extra ball-playing defender in the backline. During his eight years at Manchester City he was often the starting point for attacking moves – a quality that owes a lot to his upbringing at São Paulo, where he idolised Rogério Ceni, the top-scoring goalkeeper of all time, who hit 131 goals in his 25-year career. Luiz Batista da Silva Júnior, the club’s then youth goalkeeping coach, told the Guardian in 2018: “We had a plan to produce Ceni’s successor, so our training sessions with the boys had a big focus on footwork. Although he was very shy, Ederson paid attention to all the exercises and learned everything quickly.” In 2023 Ceni told Ederson: “I feel you are the extension of my career, how I was. I can see myself when I see you play.” In 2025 Ederson told the BBC about a ritual that helped him through his career in England. "I have one superstition. I play every game in the same boxers. Eight years, the same boxers." He left City for Fenerbahce last year.

data.name: Roger Ibáñez
data.description: The son of a Uruguayan mother and a Brazilian father – whom he used to help in the family's carpentry shop when he was a boy – Ibañez was eligible to play for Uruguay, but opted to commit to the country where he was born. Raised at Fluminense's academy, he moved to Italy in 2019 with Atalanta, then won the Conference League as a key member of José Mourinho's Roma in 2022. A year later he joined Al-Ahli for a reported €30m. One of the fastest centre-backs at the tournament, he also brings versatility, is able to operate at right-back or as a defensive midfielder. Making a World Cup squad has long been his dream, and when it finally happened, he wrote online: “This is the meaning of dream come true. In 2020 I said it openly, 2022 I kept quiet, but 2026 the story will be different. It is with great gratitude that I look back and am proud of the journey so far. Thank you to my family, my friends, my teammates who were part of this journey … God bless Brazil!"

data.name: Igor Thiago
data.description: It’s been a gruelling journey to the top. Aged 13, Igor Thiago was doing odd jobs to help keep the family afloat after the death of his father. While his mum earned a living as a street cleaner in their home city of Cidade Ocidental, he found himself being a bricklayer’s assistant, a fruit carrier at the market, and a car washer. “I had friends who wanted me to steal with them,” he told the Guardian in January. “They weren’t really friends, of course. They were acquaintances from the street, but they wanted me to use drugs, to follow the wrong path in life. Many people in my city live a reality similar to or worse than mine at the time. Many people have alcoholic parents, who use drugs, who were abandoned.” His football career sparked into life when Cruzeiro offered him a trial. He moved from there to the Bulgarian champions, Ludogorets, in 2022, then to Club Brugge, and finally to Brentford for a reported £30m in 2024. Two knee injuries meant he missed almost all his first season, but his second was stellar. “I believe I am ready,” he said. “All I know how to do in my life is score goals. God has prepared me for this moment, and if he allows me, we will bring the sixth World Cup title to Brazil.”

data.name: Rayan
data.description: In 2018 the sports outlet Globo Esporte posted online about a youngster they had spotted in Vasco da Gama’s youth system: “Take a note of this name, Vasco fans: Rayan, age 11, scored 115 goals in 2017.” Eight years later and Rayan has just arrived at his first World Cup on the back of a brilliant Premier League season with Bournemouth, and with the world at his feet. He moved to England in January for £24.7m from Vasco, where he had been since the age of six, having scored 20 goals in 57 games for Vasco in 2025. He hit the ground running in England, taking full advantage of the gap left behind at Bournemouth when Antoine Semenyo moved to Manchester City. He made his Brazil debut only in April and his future looks immense. And he has it all planned out. “I see myself, in five years, playing in Europe, for Bournemouth or another team that signs me,” he said in March. “In five years, I hope to play for the most powerful team in the world."
